text: up, and she’d used one every night she worked at Kitty’s, but the last time had been months ago…and it was never so clear as this . The hot water had pinkened her otherwise pale skin. Her hair, still wet, was several shades darker than normal, framing her heart-shaped face in a tangled mess of curls. Gently arched brows rested over her wide, blue eyes. She parted her lips as she ran the tip of a finger down the bridge of her narrow nose. Her cheekbones and collarbone were more pronounced than she remembered, but it didn’t surprise her. Food hadn’t been plentiful lately . It served as a reminder of her lingering hunger . Lara narrowed her eyes, wiping at the dirt sprinkled over her cheeks and nose. When it didn’t go away, she leaned forward to examine it. They were little spots, light brown and delicate. Freckles. Tabitha had mentioned them, sometimes, but Lara had never seen them with her own eyes . She’d been told she was beautiful many times. Other women used it to their advantage, flaunted their beauty to feed themselves. That meant doing things Lara was unwilling to do; there’d been only one time, and it would never happen again . With those painful, frustrating memories threatening to rise, she pressed her hands onto the counter. Her palms settled on soft fabric. Glancing down, she found the shirt Ronin had left. She picked up the garment and held it out in front of her. It looked huge . She pulled it on, and, surely enough, the hem fell to her knees. But that didn’t matter; it was warm and dry, much better than her other options — put her sopping wraps back on or go out there naked . Gathering her clothing from the floor, she rinsed the fabric thoroughly in the tub and hung it over the curtain rod to dry. Why did a bot have all of this? Why did they need running water, beds, and toilets ? She assumed Ronin cleaned his outer skin, but that could be accomplished easily with a rag and a bucket of water, couldn’t it ? After she finished, she opened the door and peered into the hall. Light seeped beneath the closed door at the far end. That had to be Ronin’s room . Lara returned to her room and opened her bag. She spread the packages of food out atop the drawer box — dresser , she corrected herself — and hurriedly unfolded one of them. Plucking one of the potato wedges from within, she shoved the whole thing in her mouth. It was cold and a little chewy, but tasted heavenly . She devoured five more pieces of potato, several mouthfuls of carrot, and two slices of smoked meat before she forced herself to stop . “Oh my God,” she groaned, placing her hands on her stomach and closing her eyes. It hurt to be so full . And it felt amazing . She was tempted to eat more, but she’d likely puke. After so many years of scant meals, she couldn’t stand the thought of being so deliberately wasteful. Besides, Ronin had said the food was for tonight and the next morning. She rewrapped what remained and set the bundles aside . To keep herself from thinking about food, Lara removed the treasures from her bag. She stared at them for some time, considering the best way to arrange them in their new space. Finally, she tossed her pack aside and set to work, moving some of the items several times before their placement seemed right . She frowned when she lifted the picture. Water had stained it, damaging the color in a few spots. Just another thing on the verge of destruction. It had truly become part of her world, now. She propped it up against the wall; the faces were undamaged, at least, and anything that happened to the photo couldn’t change their joy . Lara returned to the hallway. Ronin’s light was still on, the door still shut. She walked to the stairs and went down. They could work out the details of their deal another time; she wanted to explore . The only furniture in the main room was a long table with a sturdy wooden chair. A few hand tools were scattered over the table’s surface, and there was a clamp of some kind attached to it on one side . The strange wood floor was cold as she turned and padded through the wide opening directly opposite the front door. After a bit of searching, she found the light switch. The lights flickered to life and revealed another strange room . To her right there was another table, this one taller, with four chairs positioned around it. A counter ran along the walls ahead and to her left, with another section standing free in the middle of the room. She counted at least fifteen or sixteen little doors beneath the counters and up on the walls; cupboards her memory whispered, though she only had the vaguest recollection of an old woman who’d used the word when Lara was very young . Curious, she walked to the counter and opened one of the doors. Empty apart from the shelf inside. She checked a few more, finding nothing. So much space, and nothing to show for it . How much could a bot really need, though ? There was another wash basin set in the counter, with a window behind it instead of a mirror. Lara pushed the handle on the spigot up. Water poured out. No delay, no pumping, just instant water . “Best not to waste it .” Lara looked over her shoulder. Ronin stood at the entrance of the room, his chest bare. Though it appeared to be one piece, portions of his skin were lighter and darker shades, reminding her of patchwork on a blanket. She shut off the water, turned to face him,
